title: Rosehip and frankincense stretch mark oil
subtitle: A vitamin A-rich dry body oil with rosehip, sweet almond, and frankincense essential oil
excerpt: Rosehip oil is high in linoleic acid and beta-carotene, which the skin converts to vitamin A. Sweet almond oil lightens the texture so the blend absorbs without a heavy residue. Frankincense and lavender essential oils add a resinous, floral scent and are within the safe limits for leave-on body use. Yield is approximately 90 ml in an amber glass bottle.
sourceNotes: Rosehip oil (Rosa canina): rich in linoleic acid (approximately 54%) and beta-carotene; relatively short shelf life (6 to 8 months for raw oil); vitamin E addition and dark bottle storage help extend shelf life in this blend. Sweet almond oil: lightweight carrier; contains tree nuts (Prunus dulcis). Frankincense EO at 2.2%: within IFRA leave-on limits for body products. Lavender EO at 2.2%: IFRA leave-on limit for body products is 17%; well within limits. Evidence for topical rosehip oil on stretch marks: limited clinical trials show some improvement in skin elasticity (reference: Dermatologic Therapy, 2015); anecdotal support is broad but clinical evidence is moderate.

recipe.yieldDescription: Approximately 90 ml in an amber glass bottle
recipe.batchNotes: Scale by multiplying all weights. Use one 100 ml amber glass bottle per batch.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use immediately. Label with the batch date.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 6 months at room temperature; refrigerate to extend to 12 months. Rosehip oil oxidises relatively quickly; the vitamin E in this recipe slows but does not stop oxidation. Discard if the oil smells rancid or the colour darkens significantly.

glossaryTerms.definition: An omega-6 fatty acid found in high concentrations in rosehip, hemp, and evening primrose oils. Associated with skin barrier repair and moisture retention. Oils high in linoleic acid oxidise more quickly than those high in oleic acid.

content.text: Rosehip oil is valued in skincare for its

content and its high beta-carotene, which the skin converts to vitamin A. It absorbs relatively well for a rich oil and suits most skin types except very oily. The trade-off is its short shelf life: rosehip oil oxidises faster than most carrier oils and should be used fresh and stored in a dark, cool place. Adding vitamin E oil slows oxidation. Sweet almond oil lightens the blend; frankincense and lavender give a clean, resinous-floral scent at concentrations safe for a leave-on body oil. This oil contains sweet almond: patch test before use if tree nut sensitivity is a concern.

items.prepNote: Use fresh oil; rosehip oxidises quickly; buy in small quantities
Antioxidant; extends shelf life of the blend
2.2% of total oil weight; resinous, warming base note
2.2% of total oil weight; floral, calming mid note

content.text: Weigh all ingredients into a small bowl. Stir to combine. Use the funnel to decant into the amber glass bottle. Seal with the dropper or pump lid. Label with the batch date and use-by date (6 months from today).

Apply 5 to 8 drops to the skin and massage in circular motions until absorbed. Works best on damp skin after a shower. Use on the abdomen, thighs, hips, or any area of concern. For use on intact skin only; avoid broken or inflamed skin. Patch test before first use, particularly if tree nut sensitivity is a concern.

Store in the amber glass bottle in a cool, dark place. The vitamin E slows oxidation; the blend keeps 6 months at room temperature or up to 12 months refrigerated. Discard if the oil smells rancid, paint-like, or significantly darker than when first made.
